coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dot-AgOH colloid fluorescent probe for selective detection of biothiols based on the inner filter effect
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Zhou, Nan
Shi, Yanping
Sun, Chao
Zhang, Xingwei
Zhao, Wei
coredata.dc:description: Here, we present a selective and sensitive fluorescent probe for the detection and distinction of biothiols, such as glutathione (GSH) and cysteine (Cys). The adsorbance of Cys onto the surface of AgOH colloid could result in enhanced absorbance from 250 to 400 nm in the UV–vis absorption spectrum, while the addition of GSH could dissolve the AgOH colloid resulting in no change in the UV–vis absorption spectrum. Utilizing these different phenomena, two fluorescent probes were established based on the inner filter effect (IFE). The first probe, the “CDs-AgOH colloid” fluorescent probe, was used to quantitatively analyze Cys over a linear concentration range from 33 to 317 μM and a detection limit of 7.2 μM. The second probe, the “CDs-AgOH colloid-Cys” fluorescent probe, was used to quantitatively analyze GSH, with a detection limit down to 3.6 μM, and a linear range of detection of approximately 16.7 to 100 μM. The fluorescent probes were successfully applied for the detection of GSH in a fetal bovine serum (FBS) sample. Based on these results, IFE is considered to be an effective way to distinguish GSH and Cys.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Biothiols
AgOH colloid
Carbon quantum dots
Fluorescent probe
Inner filter effect

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Investigation of the feasibility of the biothiols test
Linear analysis using the probe system
Possible mechanism of Cys and GSH detection
Detection of GSH in a serum sample

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV–vis absorption (black line), fluorescence excitation (blue line) and emission (red line) spectra of CDs in aqueous solution; (b) TEM image of the as-synthesized CDs. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
(a) The FT-IR spectrum of CDs; (b) The full-scale XPS spectrum of CDs; (c) C1s, and (d) N1 s spectra of CDs;

(a) Ions selectivity analysis of carbon quantum dots; (b) Fluorescence response of the CDs system upon the addition of different metal ions; (c) the feasibility of CDs-AgOH colloid system towards biothiols; (d) CDs-Hg2+ system switching method.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs-AgOH colloid in the presence of biothiols (Cys and GSH) at pH 6; (b) pH 7 and (c) pH 8 (d) The effect of pH (6.0, 7.0, 8.0, PBS) on the fluorescence intensity of the CDs-AgOH colloid system by adding Cys and GSH.

(a) Fluorescence spectra (λex = 381 nm) of the CDs-AgOH colloid system in the presence of various amino acid (333 μM). (b) Fluorescence quenching efficiency of the CDs-AgOH colloid system in the presence of various amino acid. F0 is the fluorescence intensity of CDs-AgOH colloid. F refers to the fluorescence intensity of the CDs-AgOH system with various amino acid. (c) Fluorescence spectra (λex = 381 nm) of the CDs-AgOH colloid-Cys system in the presence of various amino acid (100 μM) (d) Fluorescence quenching efficiency of the CDs-AgOH colloid-Cys system in the presence of various amino acid. F0 is the fluorescence intensity of CDs-AgOH colloid-Cys. F refers to the fluorescence intensity of the CDs-AgOH-Cys system with various amino acid.

(a) Fluorescence response of CDs-AgOH colloid system in the presence of increasing concentration of Cys (33–317 μM). (b) Fluorescence response of CDs-AgOH colloid-Cys system in the presence of increasing concentration of GSH (16.7–100 μM). (c) Corresponding line chart of the responses of CDs-AgOH colloid system obtained in the present of various concentration of Cys, excitation at 381 nm, with a correlation coefficient R2 = 0.992. (d) A linear relationship between fluorescence intensity and GSH concentration with a correlation coefficient R2 = 0.9941. Excitation at 381 nm.

The investigation of mechanism (a) the TEM image of AgOH colloid; (b) the fluorescence spectrum; (c) the UV–vis absorption spectrum in the presence of CDs; (d) the UV–vis absorption spectrum without CDsT.
Analytical results of GSH in FBS serum sample.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dot-AgOH colloid fluorescent probe for selective detection of biothiols based on the inner filter effect

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Northeast Agricultural University, Harbin 150025, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Northeast Agricultural University, Harbin 150025, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physiology, Hei Longjiang University of Chinese Medicine, Harbin 150040, Hei Longjiang Province, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hei Longjiang University of Chinese Medicine

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physiology, Hei Longjiang University of Chinese Medicine, Harbin 150040, Hei Longjiang Province, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Here, we present a selective and sensitive fluorescent probe for the detection and distinction of biothiols, such as glutathione (GSH) and cysteine (Cys). The adsorbance of Cys onto the surface of AgOH colloid could result in enhanced absorbance from 250 to 400 nm in the UV–vis absorption spectrum, while the addition of GSH could dissolve the AgOH colloid resulting in no change in the UV–vis absorption spectrum. Utilizing these different phenomena, two fluorescent probes were established based on the inner filter effect (IFE). The first probe, the “CDs-AgOH colloid” fluorescent probe, was used to quantitatively analyze Cys over a linear concentration range from 33 to 317 μM and a detection limit of 7.2 μM. The second probe, the “CDs-AgOH colloid-Cys” fluorescent probe, was used to quantitatively analyze GSH, with a detection limit down to 3.6 μM, and a linear range of detection of approximately 16.7 to 100 μM. The fluorescent probes were successfully applied for the detection of GSH in a fetal bovine serum (FBS) sample. Based on these results, IFE is considered to be an effective way to distinguish GSH and Cys.

Mechanism of the detection of Cys and GSH.
Unlabelled Image

ce:section.ce:para: The biothiols group, an important low-mass molecule, including Cys, GSH and homocysteine (Hcy) [1], is widely distributed in living cells and tissues [2,3]. Among this group, Cys plays a crucial role in human physiological processes, such as detoxification and metabolism [4,5] among others. Abnormal Cys levels can lead to many syndromes, including child growth retardation, hair depigmentation, edema, liver damage, skin lesions and lethargy due to the loss of muscle and fat [4]. However, compared with Hcy and Cys, GSH is the most abundant cellular biothiol [6]. The detection of GSH content plays an important role in human pathological studies [7], including numerous cellular functions, such as the maintenance of intracellular redox activity and metabolism of xenobiotics [7]. More importantly, in cancer cells, especially HepG2 Cells, GSH levels are very high, being nearly 2.58 times higher than in normal cells [8,9]. Due to the importance of minute quantities of biothiols in pathological analyses and early diagnostics [10], there is an urgent need to systematically investigate the content and function of biothiols in disease diagnosis [6].

A number of methods have been exploited for the determination of biothiols, including high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [11], flow injection [12] fluorescence spectrometry [13], capillary electrophoresis [14], potentiometry [15], and mass spectrometry (MS) [16] among others. However, some of these methods have a variety of shortcomings, such as a complicated detection procedure and, time-consuming processes [17], among others. To meet the requirements for rapid diagnosis [18], sensitive and selective detection methods of biothiols need to be improved. However, owing to its simplicity, real-time detection, high sensitivity and low cost of instrumentation for sensing, fluorescent analysis is likely to be a desirable tool for biothiols detection, making it worthy of further investigation [10,17,19].

In the past few years, due to their unique optical properties, fascinating water solubility [20], chemical and photo stability and low manufacturing cost, carbon quantum dots (CDs) have gained extensive attention in contrast to traditional organic dyes and inorganic quantum dots. Given the above advantages of CDs, they have been widely used for the detection of metal ions, including Ag+ [21], Hg2+ [22], Fe3+ [23], and Cu2+ [24], among others. In biosensing, they can detect small molecules such as biothiols. For example, Hg2+ or Ag+ switching has been used to detect biothiols by “Off–On” switching. The underlying mechanism can be expressed as a significant decrease in the fluorescence intensity of the CDs system by adding Hg2+; when Cys or GSH was added to the above system, their complexation with metal ions could restore the fluorescence intensity, after which the fluorescence intensity of the CDs system containing Hg2+ was linearly recovered, permitting the quantitative analysis of biothiols. These probes can easily differentiate these biothiols from other amino acids, while it is hard to distinguish Cys, GSH and Hcy, because Cys and GSH can form complexes with metal ions in the fluorescent probe system, leadings to no changes in fluorescence emission intensity. Therefore, the development of a new method based on CDs is of great significance for the rapid and specific distinction of biothiols.

To effectively distinguish Cys and GSH, a novel fluorescent probe was established based on CDs. Firstly, the CDs were synthesized by a hydrothermal method with citric acid (CA), ethylenediamine and 1,3-propylenediamine. The fluorescent probe was constructed by combining CDs and AgOH colloids, and then the fluorescent performance of the “CD-AgOH colloid” system was investigated in the presence of different biothiols (Cys, GSH). Cys and GSH responded differently to the probe fluorescence emission intensity. Cys could quench the fluorescence intensity of the system, while GSH could slightly increase it. The experimental results validated that IFE was the possible mechanism of fluorescence quenching. Two fluorescent probes were established based on the IFE mechanism. The first was constructed as the “CDs-AgOH colloid” fluorescent probe and was used to quantitatively analyze Cys over a linear concentration range from 33 to 317 μM and detection limit of 7.2 μM. In this case, Cys could adsorb onto the surface of the “CDs-AgOH colloid” system and effectively quench the fluorescence. Probe “CDs-AgOH colloid-Cys” was established as the second probe which was used to quantitatively analyze GSH, with a detection limit down to 3.6 μM and, linear range of detection from approximately 16.7t100 μM. Under these conditions, the addition of GSH to the above probe system could cause the complete dissolution of colloid and recovery of fluorescence intensity. Moreover, this CDs-based probe was used for GSH detection in the serum sample in this study.

CA, ethylenediamine, 1,3-propanediamine and n-butyl alcohol were of analytical reagent grade and purchased from Aladdin Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Spectral pure KBr, Co(NO3)2·6H2O, ZnSO4·7H2O, KNO3, Cr(NO3)3·9H2O, AgNO3, Ni(NO3)2·6H2O, Cd(NO3)2·4H2O, Pb(NO3)2, NaCl, FeCl3·6H2O, CuCl2·2H2O, BaCl2·2H2O, MnCl2·4H2O, MgCl2·6H2O, CaCl2, HgCl2, NaH2PO4·2H2O, Na2HPO4·12H2O, alanine (Ala), serine (Ser), glutamic acid (Glu), glutamine (Gln), arginine (Arg), aspartic acid (Asp), methionine (Met), threonine (Thr), isoleucine (Ile), leucine (Leu), asparagine (Asn), histidine (His), valine (Val), lysine (Lys), proline (Pro), tryptophan (Trp), phenylalanine (Phe), glycine (Gly), cysteine (Cys) and glutathionse (GSH) were acquired from YongDa Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Tianjin, China). The abovementioned solutions were prepared using ultrapure water, and all the reagents were used directly without further purification.

Operating at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV, we obtained high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images on a FEI TF-20 microscope (FEI, Hillsboro, USA). The fluorescence spectra were acquired from a LS-55 fluorescence spectrometer (PerkinElmer, Boston, USA). The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum was obtained with a Magna-IR560 FT-IR spectrometer (Nicolet Co., Madison, WI, USA) within the range from 400 to 4000 cm−1. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) images were captured on an AXIS ULTRA DLD X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Kratos, Manchester, UK). The UV–vis absorption spectra were measured using a UV-2550 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Kratos, Japan).

Using CA as the carbon source, with ethylenediamine, and 1,3-propylenediamine as the nitrogen source, we synthesized CDs by the hydrothermal method. We dissolved 3 g of CA, 1 mL of ethylenediamine and 1 mL of 1,3-propylenediamine in 28 mL of water. Then, the mixture was transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave (50 mL), the reaction mixture was heated at 180 °C for 5 h. After the reaction was completed, dark brown products were removed from the autoclave to cool down naturally to room temperature. To remove other small impurities and most of the water, the newly synthesized CDs were fully purified with n-butanol. Finally, the prepared CDs were kept in the dark at room temperature for further use.

To distinguish the biothiols (Cys, GSH), we prepared phosphate-buffered solution (PBS) with different pH values (6.0, 7.0, 8.0). First, a suitable amount of CDs was diluted in 3 mL of ultrapure water, and then 15 μL of CDs solution was dissolved in 3 mL of the above buffer solution. Subsequently, 100 μL (0.01 M) of AgNO3 solution was added to the above solution. Finally, PBS pH = 6.0 was selected for the subsequent experiment. To investigate interference from other cations, a selective study was carried out by adding 10 μL (0.01 M) of other cations (Na+, Co2+, Zn2+, K+, Cr3+, Ag+, Ni2+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Fe3+, Cu2+, Ba2+, Mn2+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Hg2+). Then, the fluorescence emission spectra were recorded at room temperature.

Preparation of the “CDs-AgOH colloid” system was performed by repeating the above steps. The selectivity of the “CDs-AgOH colloid” system was confirmed by adding 100 μL (0.01 M) of twenty amino acids (including Ala, Ser, Glu, Gln, Arg, Asp, Met, Thr, Ile, Leu, Asn, His, Val, Lys, Pro, Trp, Phe, Gly, Cys and GSH), Based on the above experimental results, the system (CDs-AgOH colloid) was designed to distinguish Cys, GSH by the changes in the fluorescence spectra. The excitation wavelength was fixed at 381 nm and emission spectra were collected from 400 to 650 nm. The investigation of probe “CDs-AgOH colloid-Cys” was carried out under the same conditions.

To estimate the practical applicability of the probe system, the “CDs-AgOH colloid-Cys” probe system as a fluorescent probe to detect GSH was further demonstrated using an FBS serum sample. It is known that, serum samples contain numerous biological substances, such as protein, antibodies, and biothiols, among others. The serum sample was mixed with ethyl alcohol at a 1:2 radio to precipitate the protein, and after mixing for 2 min, the mixture was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 30 min. Then, the supernatant was obtained, diluted at 300-fold and detected using the above system.

The UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of the CDs were used to study the optical properties. As shown in Fig. 1a, two characteristic absorption peaks were observed at 240 nm and 345 nm. The maximum absorption wavelength of the UV–vis spectrum and excitation wavelength of the fluorescence spectrum were confirmed to be 345 nm and 381 nm, respectively. The absorption at 345 nm resulted from the n-π* transition due to the trapping of excited-state energy by the surface states [25], and the emission wavelength was 458 nm. Moreover, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was used to characterize the morphology and size of the CDs. As shown in Fig. 1b, these CDs were spherical and well-dispersed, with a diameter of approximately 4.6 nm. High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images (inset in Fig. 1b) revealed the high crystallinity [25,26] of the CDs. The lattice spacing was 0.23 nm.

The surface functional groups of the as-prepared CDs were identified by the FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 2a). According to Fig. 2a, the absorption band at 3364–3265 cm− 1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of OH and NH [27,28]. The peak at 3063 cm− 1 was attributed to CH stretching [29]. The strong absorption peak at 1639 cm− 1 indicated the existence of CO [29]. The high intensity of the peaks at 1549 cm− 1 and 1361 cm− 1 were assigned to CN and CN stretching vibrations [30,31]. These characterization results indicated the presence of numerous hydroxyl and amino groups on the surface of the as-prepared CDs, consistent with the excellent water solubility of CDs. The surface chemical and elemental compositions of the CDs were further characterized by XPS. The XPS full scan spectrum (Fig. 2b) revealed three peaks at 284.55, 399.45 and 530.75 eV, which were attributed to C1s, N1s, and O1s signals [32], respectively, demonstrating the existence of C, N and O elements in the prepared CDs. Fig. 2b presents the contents of three elements in the sample, which were calculated to be 72.28% (C), 12.67% (N), and 15.05% (O), respectively. The C1s scan spectrum (Fig. 2c) exhibited four obvious characteristic peaks at 284.55, 286.6, 288.35 and 290.45 eV, indicating the existence of C-C/C-H, CO, CO/CN, and OCO, respectively. In the N1 XPS spectrum of CDs (Fig. 2d), three peaks were observed at 399.45, 401.75 and 403.7 eV, which could respectively correspond to NH [27], aromatic N and NO. These characteristics demonstrated that the prepared CDs were carbon-rich, nitrogen-doped nanodots that, possessed multiple functional groups, for example, hydroxyl and amino groups. In conclusion, the characterization of XPS was consistent with the FT-IR results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of the feasibility of the biothiols test
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the feasibility of the CDs fluorescent probe for detecting biothiols, the interference of a variety of potentially interfering metal ions was examined by calculating the fluorescence response of the system. We selected a total of 16 different metal ions (Pb2+, Cd2+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Na+, Ba2+, Ni2+, Co2+, Hg2+, Ag+, Cr3+, Mn2+, Ca2+, Zn2+, K+, Fe3+) at a final concentration at 33.3 μM to measure their influence on the fluorescence intensity of the system. Metal ions were added to the CDs solution, and then the fluorescence spectra were recorded under excitation at 381 nm to study the effects. As shown in Fig. 3a, the addition of Hg2+ marked decreased the fluorescence intensity, and no obvious fluorescence quenching effect was detected in the presence of other ions. The above results demonstrated that the as-prepared CDs possessed high selectivity towards Hg2+ and were able to quantitatively analyze Hg2+.

Based on the method of Yan et al. [33], CDs and Hg2+ were constructed as the “CDs-Hg2+” fluorescent probe to detect biothiols, including Cys and GSH. The underlying mechanism can be expressed as “Off–On” switching fluorescence. As shown in Fig. 3b, the addition of 50 μM Hg2+ to the CDs solution resulted in a sharp decrease in fluorescence intensity. After addition of the same amount of 50 μM Cys and 50 μM GSH to the above solution, the fluorescence intensity was restored to almost the same extent, and the recovery ratio was 93.8% and 95.8%, respectively. Therefore, the CDs-Hg2+ probe system allowed the quantitative analysis of biothiols, but it was unable to distinguish GSH from Cys, in agreement with previous reports [34].

The addition of Cys and GSH to the “CDs-AgOH colloid” solution resulted in markedly different experimental phenomena. Thus, we designed the “CDs-AgOH colloid” probe system to effectively distinguish GSH and Cys. To the solution of CDs prepared in PBS was added 333 μM AgNO3 solution to form the “CDs-AgOH colloid” system, which was slightly muddy and showed a slight decrease in fluorescence intensity. The same amount of Cys and GSH was then added to the above system. The fluorescence intensity increased slightly with the addition of GSH and decreased sharply with the addition of Cys. Thus, the system provided the possibility of quantitatively analyzing Cys and GSH. In comparison to the fluorescent probe system containing Hg2+, the probe with Ag+ possessed better selectivity. Many reports [35,36] have shown that the carbon quantum dots‑silver ion switching method can be used as a probe to detect biothiols. The underlying mechanism was similar to that of Hg2+ “Off–On” switching, which was achieved by Ag+ competition with carbon quantum dots, which could not distinguish biothiols (Cys, GSH). Based on the above results, the “CDs-AgOH colloid” system had the potential ability to allow selective and quantitative analyses of Cys and GSH. Therefore, the “CDs-AgOH colloid” system was selected as the fluorescent probe for the detection of biothiols.

Given the different fluorescence responses of Cys and GSH to the probe system, we explored other potential external parameters that might impact the probe system, such as the pH value. At room temperature, the different performances in detecting biothiols in PBS pH 6.0–8.0 PBS are shown in Fig. 4 . Fig. 4a, b and c show the fluorescence responses of Cys and GSH at pH 6.0, 7.0 and 8.0, respectively. As shown in Fig. 4d, pH 6.0 would be the best choice for further investigations owing to the opposite changes after addition of Cys and GSH, respectively. The former led to fluorescence quenching and the latter to fluorescence recovery in the probe system. The following selective experiments were conducted under this condition.

To investigate the selectivity of the CDs-AgOH system for various amino acids, 333 μM of different amino acids including Ala, Ser, Glu, Gln, Arg, Asp, Met, Thr, Ile, Leu, Asn, His, Val, Lys, Pro, Trp, Phe, Gly, Cys and GSH, were added to the CDs-AgOH system to study their effects on the fluorescent probe in Fig. 5a. Compared with GSH and Cys, other amino acids showed no obvious changes in fluorescence spectra. Fig. 5b shows the fluorescence quenching efficiencies of various amino acids, indicating that the system possessed a certain selectivity for GSH and Cys.

The fluorescence was still responsive when Cys and GSH coexisted. Therefore, to further investigate the detection of GSH in the presence of Cys, we designed a new system denoted “CDs-AgOH colloid-Cys”, and the results are shown in Fig. 5c. based on the CDs-AgOH colloid system, 167 μM Cys was added to form the “CDs-AgOH colloid-Cys” fluorescent probe. After adding the above-mentioned amino acids (100 μM) to this fluorescent probe system, only GSH caused a sharp fluorescence recovery. When Cys and GSH coexisted at the same concentration, the degree of fluorescence recovery was slightly higher than when GSH was present alone. This result revealed the interference of Cys in the detection of GSH, but the effect was not significant. Thus, the “CDs-AgOH colloid-Cys” system exhibited excellent selectivity towards GSH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Linear analysis using the probe system
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimized conditions, we explored the application of the “CDs-AgOH colloid” system as a fluorescent probe to detect Cys. As shown in Fig. 6a, when different concentrations of Cys, ranging from 33 to 317 μM, were added to the CDs-AgOH colloid system, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs-AgOH colloid system increased linearly at 458 nm. Fig. 6a shows a good linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity and the concentration of Cys. As illustrated in the inset in Fig. 6a, the linear equation was expressed as Y = −0.55791X + 663.60471, where Y was the fluorescence intensity, and X was the concentration of Cys (μM). The correlation coefficient was 0.99143 and limitation of detection was 7.2 μM. These results showed that the as-designed “CDs-AgOH colloid” system could be used to quantitatively detect Cys.

To obtain a linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity and GSH concentration, the other fluorescent probe system “CDs-AgOH colloid-Cys” was explored. Fig. 6b shows the relationship between both of them. The fluorescence intensity gradually increased along with the increase in GSH at a concentration from 16.7–100 μM. When the GSH concentration reached 100 μM, the fluorescence intensity did not increase linearly. As evident in the inset in Fig. 6b, various concentrations of GSH were added to the “CDs-AgOH colloid-Cys” system, and the fluorescence intensity was measured to investigate the sensitivity of “CDs-AgOH colloid-Cys” for GSH detection. The linearity between the fluorescence intensity and GSH concentration was: Y = 1.12189X + 607.8067, where Y is the fluorescence intensity, X is the concentration of GSH (μM), and 3.6 μM is the limitation of detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible mechanism of Cys and GSH detection
ce:section.ce:para: To further clarify the mechanism of biothiols detection, a series of experiments were conducted. First, we analyzed the AgOH colloid by electron microscopy. The results are shown in Fig. 7a. The TEM image illustrated that the diameter of the complex was approximately 50 nm, which was consistent with the size of the colloid. The addition of Ag+ to the PBS resulted in the formation of AgOH colloid. The UV–vis spectra of five systems (CDs, CDs-AgOH colloid, CDs-AgOH colloid + Cys, CDs-AgOH colloid + GSH, CDs-AgOH colloid + Cys supernatant) were analyzed, and corresponding fluorescence spectra were also detected. By comparing with the fluorescence and UV–vis spectra of CDs, it was found that the absorption spectra of the AgOH colloid increased slightly at 345 nm, indicating that Ag+ ions coordinated primarily with CDs and formed the “CDs-AgOH colloid” system, which led to the slight decrease in emission intensity of the corresponding fluorescence spectra. Following the addition of GSH to the “CDs-AgOH colloid” system, the UV–vis absorption intensity decreased to the intensity observed without AgOH colloid. Simultaneously, the system became clear. These phenomena might be attributed to the competitive combination of Ag+ ion arising from GSH, leading to the dissolution of AgOH colloid and subsequent slight increase in the corresponding fluorescence intensity. The UV–vis absorption intensity increased markedly from 250 to 400 nm with the addition of Cys to the “CDs-AgOH colloid” system, which indicated that the excitation light was absorbed by AgOH colloid-Cys, causing a decreased fluorescence intensity of the “CDs-AgOH colloid” system. After centrifugation, the UV–vis absorption and fluorescence intensity of the supernatant were recovered to the extent of CDs without AgOH.

To investigate the mechanism responsible for the interaction between Cys and AgOH colloids, we mixed Cys and AgOH colloids without CDs. Strong absorption in the UV–vis spectrum was detected at 345 nm, confirming that the possible mechanism was IFE. The so-called IFE indicates that the absorption spectrum of the absorber in the detection system can coincide with the fluorophore excitation or emission spectrum, and the absorber can absorb the excitation or emission light [37]. To verify this mechanism, the absorption of the absorber ranged from 300 to 400 nm and the excitation of the CDs from 300 to 410 nm, which were basically overlapping. Therefore, the possible mechanism was confirmed to be IFE.

The mechanism responsible for the whole reaction is shown in the graphical abstract. The CDs solution absorbed at 345 nm and emitted at 381 nm. Following the addition of AgNO3 solution was added, the AgOH colloid formed and fluorescence intensity slightly decreased. When Cys was added to the above solution, it adsorbed onto the AgOH colloid surface and absorbed at 345 nm, resulting in the decrease in fluorescence intensity via the IFE mechanism. By centrifugal separation, the AgOH colloid was removed from the solution, and the fluorescence spectrum was restored almost to the intensity of CDs. The reaction was thus further validated to occur via the IFE mechanism. With the direct addition of GSH to the above solution without centrifugal separation, the AgOH colloid dissolved, and the fluorescence was recovered. The addition of GSH alone to the CDs-AgOH colloid system caused the AgOH colloid to dissolve and the fluorescence intensity to slightly recover. In conclusion, the CDs-AgOH colloid system could distinguish GSH and Cys due to their different binding capacities to Ag+ ions. As shown in the graphic abstract, several –OH, –NH3, and –SH groups are present in the structure of GSH [1] exceeding the number in Cys (only one –OH, one –NH3 and one –SH). Therefore, GSH exhibited a much greater binding capacity to Ag+ ions than Cys. Consequently, Cys could bind to the surface of the AgOH colloid to form a stable complex structure, which resulted in the absorption from 250 to 400 nm, while GSH could lead to dissolution of the AgOH colloid. In conclusion, the addition of Cys and GSH had different effects on the fluorescence emission intensity of the CDs-AgOH system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of GSH in a serum sample
ce:section.ce:para: Considering the above results, application of the “CDs-AgOH colloid-Cys” probe for GSH detection in serum was investigated, the serum sample was purchased from Zhejiang Tianhang Biotechnology Co., Ltd., frozen at −20 °C. After simple removal of the protein, we diluted 300 times to ensure GSH concentration fit the linear detection requirement and was used to detect GSH, with different concentrations of GSH were added to the probe solution, then, corresponding fluorescence responses of probe at 460 nm for GSH in real samples were collected, the results were expressed in Table 1 and obtained recoveries ranging from 96.3% to 102.6%, thus, the synthetized CDs could be designed as “CDs-AgOH colloid-Cys” fluorescent probe and could detect GSH in serum sample.

In this work, a type of CDs was synthesized using citric acid, ethylenediamine and 1,3-propanediamine via the hydrothermal method. We constructed the probes “CDs-AgOH colloid” and “CDs-AgOH colloid-Cys” based on “IFE effect” to distinguish Cys and GSH, with a detection limit of 7.2 μM and 3.6 μM; and detection range from 33 to 317 μM and 16.7–100 μM. Moreover, the fluorescent sensor system “CDs-AgOH colloid-Cys” was successfully applied for GSH detection in a serum sample, supporting the potential application of the probe system as an excellent fluorescent probe for quantitative detection of Cys and GSH.
Conceptualization: Nan Zhou and Yanping Shi.
Data curation: Yanping Shi and Chao Sun.
Formal analysis: Yanping Shi and Xingwei Zhao.
Funding acquisition: Nan Zhou and Wei Zhao.
Investigation: Yanping Shi and Nan Zhou.
Methodology: Yanping Shi and Nan Zhou.
Project administration: Nan Zhou and Wei Zhao.
Resources: Nan Zhou and Wei Zhao.
Software: Yanping Shi.
Supervision: Nan Zhou and Yanping Shi.
Validation: Yanping Shi.
Visualization: Yanping Shi.
Roles/Writing - original draft: Yanping Shi.
Writing - review & editing: Yanping Shi.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to express their sincere thanks to the Analytical and Testing Centre of Northeast Agricultural University.
